findings.description: Off-by-one in stock guard: `row["stock"] > quantity` uses strict greater-than, so a reservation for exactly the available stock (e.g., stock=5, quantity=5) returns False and silently rejects a fully fulfillable order.

No validation that `quantity` is a positive integer in `reserve`: a zero or negative value always passes the stock guard, commits an UPDATE that adds (not subtracts) stock, and returns True — callers can silently corrupt inventory.

Race condition / TOCTOU in `reserve`: the SELECT and UPDATE are not wrapped in a single `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction, so two concurrent callers can both read sufficient stock and both commit, overselling inventory.
`restock` silently succeeds (returns None, commits) even when the SKU does not exist; `cursor.rowcount` is never checked, so callers have no way to detect a restock operation that affected zero rows.
`restock` accepts any integer `quantity`, including zero and negative values, which would silently decrement stock via `stock + ?` without any guard or error.

In every public method, `self._connect()` is called before the `try` block; if `_connect()` raises (e.g., file not found, permissions error), `conn` is unbound and the `finally: conn.close()` will raise `UnboundLocalError`, masking the original exception — affects lines 29, 41, 61, 74, and 96.

SQL injection in `search_by_name`: `fragment` is interpolated directly into the query string via an f-string (`f"WHERE name LIKE '%{fragment}%'"`); any attacker-controlled input can execute arbitrary SQL — defer detail to critic-security.

N+1 query pattern in `low_stock_report`: all products are fetched in one query, then a second per-SKU COUNT query is issued inside the loop for each low-stock item — defer detail to critic-performance.
